section.title: Head Coverings in Worship
section.sectionRationale: Turning to the conduct of public worship, Paul commends the Corinthians for holding the traditions and then corrects their practice of head coverings. He grounds his instruction in an order of headship, God, Christ, man, and woman, and in the creation account, yet he balances it at once: in the Lord man and woman are interdependent, and everything comes from God. The passage teaches an abiding truth about honor, order, and the glad distinction of the sexes in worship, expressed in a first-century custom, and it calls the church to worship in a way that honors God and one another.
section.thesis: In public worship the order of headship, God over Christ, Christ over man, man over woman, is to be honored, so that men and women worship in ways that bring honor rather than shame; yet in the Lord man and woman are mutually dependent, for woman came from man and man is born of woman, and everything comes from God.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the order of headship and the honor of covered or uncovered heads in worship; man as the image and glory of God, woman the glory of man, and the sign of authority; and the balancing truth that in the Lord man and woman are interdependent, everything coming from God.

verses.text: Now I commend you for remembering me in everything and for maintaining the traditions, just as I passed them on to you.
But I want you to understand that the {{kephale|head}} of every man is Christ, and the head of the woman is man, and the head of Christ is God.
Every man who prays or prophesies with his head covered dishonors his head.
And every woman who prays or prophesies with her head uncovered dishonors her head, for it is just as if her head were shaved.
If a woman does not cover her head, she should have her hair cut off. And if it is shameful for a woman to have her hair cut or shaved off, she should cover her head.
A man ought not to cover his head, since he is the {{eikon|image}} and {{doxa|glory}} of God; but the woman is the glory of man.
For man did not come from woman, but woman from man.
Neither was man created for woman, but woman for man.
For this reason a woman ought to have a sign of {{exousia|authority}} on her head, because of the angels.
In the Lord, however, woman is not independent of man, nor is man independent of woman.
For just as woman came from man, so also man is born of woman. But everything comes from God.
Judge for yourselves: Is it proper for a woman to pray to God with her head uncovered?
Doesn’t nature itself teach you that if a man has long hair, it is a disgrace to him,
but that if a woman has long hair, it is her glory? For long hair is given to her as a covering.
If anyone is inclined to dispute this, we have no other practice, nor do the churches of God.

kephale.gloss: Head. Used here in a relational sense within an ordered series, God, Christ, man, woman. Interpreters weigh whether it stresses authority or source, or both together, but the term sets a pattern of honor and order that the Trinity itself displays, since even Christ has God as His head without any lessening of His deity.

eikon.gloss: Image, a visible representation that reflects its original. Man is called the image and glory of God, echoing the creation of humanity in God's image, so that in worship he is to reflect God's honor. The word ties the passage back to Genesis and to the dignity of bearing God's likeness.

doxa.gloss: Glory, honor, that which displays the worth of another. Man is the glory of God and woman the glory of man, and a woman's long hair is her glory. The word runs through the passage as its key idea: worship is to render honor rightly, each reflecting glory in the place God has given.

exousia.gloss: Authority. The woman is to have a sign of authority on her head. Read as authority she is under, it marks her place in the ordered household of worship; read as her own authority to pray and prophesy, it dignifies her active part in the assembly. Either way it is a mark of her rightful standing in worship.

basic.context: Paul now takes up how the church behaves in public worship, beginning with head coverings for men and women. He commends the Corinthians for keeping the traditions he handed on, then teaches an order of relationships: the head of every man is Christ, the head of woman is man, and the head of Christ is God. In the culture of the day, a man praying with his head covered and a woman praying with her head uncovered each brought shame, so Paul urges them to worship in a way that honors this order. He roots it in creation, man made in God's image, woman made from and for man, yet he immediately balances it: in the Lord neither is independent of the other, and everything ultimately comes from God. Notably, women pray and prophesy in the assembly; the question is how, not whether.
basic.hermeneutics: This is one of the harder passages to apply, because it wraps an abiding principle inside a specific first-century custom. The enduring truths are the order of headship, the glad distinction of men and women, and worship that honors God and one another. Faithful Christians differ on whether the covering itself binds the church today or whether it was the cultural expression of a principle that now takes other forms. Paul himself invites the church to judge for yourselves and appeals to what honors God.
basic.originalLanguages: The key term is kephale, head, set in an ordered series from God to Christ to man to woman. Man is called the eikon, image, and doxa, glory, of God, echoing Genesis, while woman is the glory of man. A woman is to have a sign of exousia, authority, on her head, whether the authority she stands under or her own authority to take part. Through it all runs doxa, glory or honor, the passage's central concern.
basic.typology: The language of image and glory reaches back to Genesis, where humanity is made in God's image. Christ, the true image of God, restores that likeness, and the ordered relationships of worship reflect in a creaturely way the order within the Godhead, where the Son honors the Father as His head without any loss of equal deity.
basic.christ: Christ stands within the very order Paul describes: the head of Christ is God, yet the Son is fully divine, equal with the Father, and honors Him freely. This dismantles any idea that being under a head means being lesser in worth. As man is called to honor Christ his head and woman to honor rightly in worship, all of it is patterned on the Son who gladly honors the Father, and it points to Christ the true image of God in whom our dignity is restored.
basic.god: God is the source and goal of the whole order: the head of Christ is God, and everything comes from God. He is not a God of confusion but of honor and order, and He has made men and women different and interdependent for His glory. The passage guards both the dignity of God's image in humanity and the beauty of the distinctions He built into creation.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis tells of humanity made in God's image and of woman formed from man. Later in this letter Paul says God is not a God of disorder but of peace in the assemblies. Galatians declares that in Christ there is neither male nor female, all one in Him. Ephesians speaks of headship shaped by self-giving love.
basic.oneStory: From creation God made humanity in His image, male and female, distinct and made for one another. Sin twisted these relationships into domination and rivalry, but in Christ they are being restored to honor and mutual dependence, all things coming from God. This passage, for all its cultural distance, belongs to that story of a people learning again to worship in a way that reflects the honor and order of God Himself.

context.literary: The passage begins a new section on public worship that runs through the Lord's Supper and spiritual gifts. It is framed by commendation in verse 2 and an appeal to common church practice in verse 16, and it moves through an ordered statement of headship, an argument from creation about image and glory, and a deliberate counterbalance affirming interdependence in the Lord, ending with appeals to propriety and to nature.
context.historical: In Roman Corinth, head coverings and hairstyles carried strong signals of gender, status, and respectability in public and religious settings. For a woman to pray uncovered, or for a man to cover his head as in some Roman rites, could suggest impropriety or blur the honorable distinctions of the sexes. Paul addresses a live concern about how worship appeared to the church and to outsiders, that it honor God and give no needless offense.
context.canonical: The passage joins the biblical thread on humanity created in God's image, male and female, and on ordered yet loving relationships in the household of God. It stands alongside Paul's insistence elsewhere that worship be orderly and honorable, and it must be read with Galatians 3:28 and with the mutuality of verses 11 and 12, which guard the equal dignity of men and women in Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To correct disorder in Corinthian worship by grounding the honorable distinction of men and women in an order of headship and in creation, while carefully balancing this with the interdependence of the sexes in the Lord, so that the church worships in a way that honors God and reflects His design.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The abiding principles are prescriptive: the order of headship, the dignity and distinction of men and women, and worship that honors God and one another. The specific practice of a physical covering is where interpreters differ, some holding it binding, others reading it as the cultural expression of a principle that endures in changing forms.

debates.question: Does the head covering itself bind the church today, or was it the cultural expression of an abiding principle?

views.label: A culturally expressed principle
views.case: Paul roots the covering in what was honorable and shameful in that setting and appeals to nature and propriety; the enduring point is honoring the order and distinction God made, which today may be expressed in ways other than a literal veil.

views.label: An abiding symbolic practice
views.case: Others hold that Paul grounds the covering not merely in custom but in creation and the angels, so the symbol itself carries a lasting significance the church should continue to observe.

debates.bearing: Both readings honor Paul's central concern, the God-given order and distinction of men and women in worship; they differ on whether the veil is the permanent form or a cultural expression of that principle.

debates.question: What does head, kephale, chiefly mean in verse 3?

views.label: Authority and order
views.case: The ordered series and the language of honor and shame suggest a relationship of authority, patterned on the Son who honors the Father as His head, without implying inferiority of being.

views.label: Source or origin
views.case: Others note that verses 8 and 12 speak of woman coming from man and man born of woman, so head may carry the sense of source, stressing origin and relationship more than rank.

debates.bearing: On either reading the passage teaches ordered, honoring relationships that reflect God's design, safeguarded from any notion of unequal worth by verses 11 and 12.

study.originalLanguages: The passage turns on kephale, head, arranged in a series, God the head of Christ, Christ the head of man, man the head of woman, a pattern of honor and order rather than of unequal worth, since the Son who has God as His head is fully God. Man is the eikon, image, and doxa, glory, of God, reaching back to Genesis, while woman is the glory of man. The woman is to bear a sign of exousia, authority, on her head, whether the authority she is under or her own authority to pray and prophesy. Binding it all together is doxa, glory, the honor that worship is meant to render rightly.

typology.type: Man as the image and glory of God
typology.fulfillment: Reaches back to humanity made in God's image in Genesis and forward to Christ, the true image of God, in whom that likeness and dignity are restored in His people.
Reflects in a creaturely way the order within the Godhead, where the Son honors the Father as His head without any loss of equal deity, the pattern for honoring relationships in worship.

study.christ: Christ is woven into the very structure of the passage: the head of Christ is God. The Son, fully equal with the Father in deity, freely honors Him as His head, which forbids us from reading headship as a mark of lesser worth. So the honoring of one's head in worship, for man and woman alike, is patterned on Christ's own glad honoring of the Father. And Christ is the true image of God, the one in whom the dignity first given to humanity is renewed, so that all our worship and all our relationships find their measure in Him.
study.god: God is the fountain and end of the whole order: the head of Christ is God, and everything comes from God. He made humanity in His image, male and female, distinct and interdependent, and He is honored when His people worship according to that design. He is a God of order and peace, not of confusion, and this passage guards both the dignity of His image in every person and the goodness of the distinctions He built into creation, all for His glory.

crossReferences.note: Humanity created in God's image, male and female, behind the language of image and glory in verse 7.
Woman formed from man and for him as a fitting helper, the background to verses 8 and 9.
In Christ there is neither male nor female; equal dignity that must be held with the mutuality of verses 11 and 12.
God is not a God of disorder but of peace, the concern for honorable worship that shapes this passage.
Headship defined by Christ's self-giving love for the church, guarding against any domineering reading.

study.oneStory: God made humanity in His image, male and female, distinct and made for one another, and the fall twisted these relationships into rivalry and domination. In Christ they are being healed into honor and glad interdependence, everything coming from God. This passage, however bound to its time, belongs to that larger story: a redeemed people learning to worship in a way that reflects the honor and order of God Himself, until worship is made perfect in the presence of Christ, the true image of God.

quiz.q: What order of headship does Paul set out in verse 3?

options.text: God is the head of Christ, Christ of man, and man of woman.
options.note: Paul names an ordered series from God to Christ to man to woman.

options.text: Christ is the head of man, and man the head of woman.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but it omits the crowning link, that the head of Christ is God.

options.text: There is no order; all relationships are identical.
options.note: Paul does teach an order, while also affirming interdependence.

options.text: Man is the head of God, Christ, and woman.
options.note: This reverses the order; God is the head of Christ.

quiz.q: How does the phrase the head of Christ is God guard against misreading headship?

options.text: It shows headship need not mean lesser worth, since the Son is fully God yet honors the Father.
options.note: Christ's equal deity within the order rules out any idea of inferiority.

options.text: It models headship as a relationship of honor rather than domination.
options.note: A true implication, resting on the deeper point that the Son is fully equal in deity.

options.text: It has nothing to do with how we understand headship.
options.note: It is precisely the pattern that shapes a right reading of headship.

options.text: It proves Christ is inferior to the Father in His being.
options.note: Orthodox faith confesses the Son as fully equal in deity.

quiz.q: How does Paul balance the order of headship in verses 11 and 12?

options.text: In the Lord man and woman are interdependent, and everything comes from God.
options.note: Woman came from man, man is born of woman, all from God.

options.text: By stressing that man too is born of woman, so neither can boast.
options.note: True to verse 12, though the full balance adds that everything comes from God.

options.text: By saying women are independent of men entirely.
options.note: He says neither is independent of the other.

options.text: By declaring the order of headship abolished.
options.note: He balances the order with interdependence, not abolishes it.

quiz.q: Which is a fair way to handle the head covering itself for today?

options.text: Hold firmly to the principle of honoring God's order and distinction, while recognizing Christians differ on the covering's binding form.
options.note: The abiding principle is clear; the form is where the church debates fairly.

options.text: Continue the covering itself, since Paul grounds it in creation and the angels.
options.note: A respectable orthodox reading; others hold the veil was the cultural expression of the abiding principle.

options.text: Insist everyone must agree the veil is merely cultural.
options.note: Orthodox readers differ; humility here is fitting.

options.text: Treat head coverings as the heart of the gospel.
options.note: The custom is not the gospel; the principle serves honorable worship.

quiz.q: According to the passage, do women take an active part in public worship?

options.text: Yes; women pray and prophesy in the assembly, and the question is how, not whether.
options.note: Verse 5 assumes women praying and prophesying; Paul addresses the manner.

options.text: Yes, though the manner is to be weighed with Paul's other instructions on order.
options.note: Rightly affirms their participation; this passage regulates the how, to be read alongside his wider teaching.

options.text: No; women are entirely silent here.
options.note: This passage assumes women praying and prophesying.

options.text: Only men may pray or prophesy.
options.note: Verse 5 speaks of women who pray or prophesy.

questions: Paul commends the Corinthians for holding the traditions he handed on. What faithful teaching have you received that you are called to keep and pass on?
The head of Christ is God, yet the Son is fully equal with the Father. How does Christ's glad honoring of the Father reshape the way you think about order and honor among God's people?
In the Lord man and woman are not independent of one another, and everything comes from God. Where might pride or rivalry between the sexes need to give way to glad interdependence?
This passage wraps a lasting principle in a cultural custom. How can you hold firmly to what honors God while showing humility toward fellow Christians who apply the details differently?
Worship is meant to render honor rightly to God and one another. What in your own worship, in attitude or action, might God be calling you to bring into greater honor and order?

translationNotes.note: Head translates kephale, used relationally in an ordered series; interpreters weigh whether it stresses authority or source, but the pattern is one of honor, safeguarded from any idea of unequal worth by the head of Christ being God.

The verse assumes women praying and prophesying in the gathering; Paul is regulating the manner of their participation, not forbidding it, which must be weighed alongside his instructions elsewhere on order in worship.

A sign of authority on her head can be read either as the authority she stands under or as her own authority to pray and prophesy; the reference to the angels likely concerns due order and reverence in worship.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Corinthians 11:2-16. Any em dashes in the source have been rendered as commas to comply with the no-dash style rule; wording is unchanged.
